does: This paper identifies and addresses a critical gap in healthcare AI evaluation by developing benchmarks that measure reliability, safety, and clinical relevance under real-world conditions rather than isolated task performance. The authors demonstrate that while frontier models achieve near-perfect scores on medical licensing exams, their performance degrades substantially on actual clinical workflows, with documented drops to 0.74–0.85 on documentation tasks, 0.61–0.76 on clinical decision support, and lower scores on other critical activities. The work establishes that existing ad hoc benchmarks optimized for narrow task performance fail to capture the complexity and failure modes present in live clinical environments, where AI systems must operate reliably across multimodal inputs, diverse patient populations, and interconnected clinical processes. The paper advocates for systematic benchmarking methodologies that evaluate generative, multimodal, and agentic AI systems against the actual demands of clinical practice rather than academic performance metrics.
connects: This work extends decades of medical AI evaluation literature by moving beyond the knowledge-assessment paradigm that dominated earlier benchmarking efforts, such as licensing exam performance metrics, toward pragmatic measurement of clinical task execution. It builds on recent insights from AI safety and robustness research showing that capability assessments in controlled settings often fail to predict real-world performance, particularly in high-stakes domains. The paper connects to emerging work on multimodal AI evaluation that considers the integration of text, images, and structured data in clinical contexts, as well as agentic AI assessment frameworks that measure sequential decision-making under uncertainty. It responds to growing recognition in healthcare ML that deployment readiness requires measuring reliability across diverse patient populations, edge cases, and workflow integration points that standard curated datasets systematically exclude.
enables: This benchmarking framework enables future development of more clinically grounded AI systems by providing concrete, reproducible measurement standards for healthcare deployment decisions. It creates pathways for systematic evaluation of reliability improvements, safety interventions, and failure mode mitigation strategies across real clinical tasks. The work supports development of domain-specific metrics and validation protocols that could become industry standards for clinical AI approval and monitoring, facilitating more rigorous regulatory assessment and clinical adoption. By establishing baseline performance gaps between controlled and real-world settings, this research enables targeted research into bridging those gaps through improved model architectures, training procedures, and clinical integration strategies. The framework also enables comparative evaluation of different AI approaches (generative versus traditional, single-task versus multimodal) on clinically meaningful dimensions, driving more evidence-based selection and deployment of AI tools in healthcare organizations.
